text:
GENITOURINARY MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Raymond Kowalski
DOB: [on file]
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Division: Genitourinary Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old Polish-American male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, presenting for scheduled follow-up after completing cycle 3 of carboplatin + docetaxel chemotherapy. He is here today for toxicity assessment, laboratory review, and disease status evaluation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski has a well-documented history of high-risk prostate adenocarcinoma initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago at age 68. At diagnosis, he had a PSA of 187.4 ng/mL, Gleason score 9 (5+4) / Grade Group 5 disease with clinical T3bN1M1b staging, including pelvic lymph node involvement and bone metastases at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala. He was initiated on ADT with leuprolide plus abiraterone/prednisone as first-line therapy for metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), achieving a nadir PSA of 0.8 ng/mL with sclerotic healing of known bone lesions.

He subsequently developed castration resistance approximately 24 months into his course, with PSA rising to 8.6 ng/mL and confirmed castrate testosterone. Germline testing performed during the hormone-sensitive period revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT), and somatic NGS of the L3 vertebral metastasis at the time of CRPC progression showed biallelic BRCA2 loss (LOH plus somatic deletion), TP53 p.R175H, AR amplification (~8x), and RB1 homozygous deletion. MSI was stable and TMB was low (4 mutations/Mb).

He received enzalutamide 160 mg daily as second-line AR pathway inhibitor, achieving an initial PSA response (nadir 1.9 ng/mL) before progressing radiographically at approximately 7 months, with new bone lesions at the right femoral neck, T11, left acetabulum, and new pelvic lymph node involvement on PSMA PET/CT. He was then started on olaparib 300 mg BID given his BRCA2 biallelic loss, which yielded a 69% PSA decline (from 18.2 to 5.7 ng/mL) and partial radiographic response in bone. However, his course on olaparib was complicated by recurrent, transfusion-dependent anemia requiring two dose reductions (300 → 200 → 150 mg BID) and two PRBC transfusions. Despite darbepoetin alfa support and dose reduction to 150 mg BID, he developed a second episode of Grade 3 anemia and Grade 2 thrombocytopenia at 9 months, requiring olaparib to be held again. At 11 months on olaparib, restaging PSMA PET/CT demonstrated visceral progression with a new 1.4 cm PSMA-avid hepatic lesion (segment VII) and 0.9 cm left lower lobe pulmonary nodule, along with new osseous lesions and re-enlarging pelvic lymph nodes. PSA at that point was 31.7 ng/mL. Olaparib was discontinued.

Given visceral metastases and BRCA2 biallelic loss, the decision was made to proceed with platinum-based combination chemotherapy — docetaxel 60 mg/m2 plus carboplatin AUC 4, every 21 days — with G-CSF (filgrastim) prophylaxis given his prior myelosuppression history and ECOG PS 2. He received cycle 1 without hospitalization, though he developed Grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 0.6 K/uL on day 12) managed with additional filgrastim, as well as Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy (bilateral feet, worsening on a background of pre-existing Grade 1 neuropathy), Grade 2 alopecia, and Grade 2 nausea. Docetaxel was dose-reduced to 55 mg/m2 for cycle 2 and maintained since. Restaging CT after 2 cycles demonstrated a mixed response: the hepatic segment VII lesion shrank from 1.4 to 0.8 cm, the pulmonary nodule was stable, bone lesions were stable to minimally improved, and pelvic lymph nodes decreased. PSA after cycle 2 was 18.4 ng/mL.

He has now completed cycle 3 (docetaxel 55 mg/m2 + carboplatin AUC 4) and presents today for assessment.

RESPONSE/CURRENT DISEASE STATUS: Mr. Kowalski's PSA today returns at 12.1 ng/mL, representing a continued decline from the pre-chemotherapy nadir of 31.7 ng/mL and translating to approximately a 62% reduction from that peak. This is encouraging, though not a complete response, and his performance status has not recovered to his pre-visceral metastasis baseline. He continues on leuprolide 22.5 mg IM every 3 months to maintain castrate testosterone suppression, and denosumab 120 mg SC monthly for bone-targeted therapy (initiated at the time of enzalutamide progression). He is on calcium 500 mg and vitamin D 400 IU supplementation.

CURRENT SYMPTOMS: He endorses Grade 2 fatigue — persistent and somewhat limiting, though improved since the most recent transfusion approximately 6 weeks ago. He continues to experience numbness and tingling in both feet and now also in both hands, which he describes as worse than it was before starting chemotherapy. He is wearing soft-soled shoes and reports difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning clothing). His right upper quadrant discomfort, which was present at the start of chemotherapy and attributed to the hepatic metastasis, has resolved. He denies new bone pain, headache, visual changes, shortness of breath at rest (though exertional dyspnea with stairs is present), fevers, or infectious symptoms. He reports stable appetite and weight.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Fatigue (Grade 2), bilateral hand and foot paresthesias/numbness (Grade 2), exertional dyspnea (Grade 1, improved), mild hair loss (pre-existing since chemotherapy initiation, Grade 2 alopecia). Hot flashes ongoing (pre-existing from long-term ADT).
Negative: No fever, chills, night sweats, chest pain, palpitations. No nausea/vomiting (nausea well-controlled with antiemetics). No abdominal pain. No hematuria, dysuria, or urinary obstruction symptoms. No new bone pain or back pain at rest (occasional dull ache in lower back, 1/10, unchanged). No focal neurologic deficits, no falls. No oral mucositis or skin rash. No edema.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: BP 122/76 mmHg, HR 82 bpm (regular), RR 16/min, Temp 98.1°F (36.7°C), SpO2 96% on room air, Weight 171 lbs (BMI 25.8 — modest weight loss compared to baseline BMI 26.4). Pain: 1/10 (low back, baseline).

General: Fatigued-appearing male, alert and oriented x3, cooperative, no acute distress. Appears older than stated age. Alopecia present.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: PERRL, no scleral icterus or conjunctival pallor today, mucous membranes moist, no oral mucositis.

Neck: Supple, no palpable lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular regions.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Normal respiratory effort.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No S3 or S4. No peripheral edema in the lower extremities.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender to palpation throughout. No guarding or rigidity. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly on exam today (previously tender in the right upper quadrant; now resolved). Normoactive bowel sounds.

Musculoskeletal: No visible joint swelling. Gait is slightly antalgic but patient ambulates without assistive device today (uses cane at home per report). No focal bone tenderness on percussion of the spine.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 bilaterally in upper extremities and proximal lower extremities. Distal lower extremity strength 4/5 bilaterally (dorsiflexion). Sensory exam: Reduced light-touch sensation in bilateral feet (stocking distribution, extending to mid-calf), mild reduction in fingertip sensation bilaterally. Vibration sense reduced at toes bilaterally. Romberg mildly positive. DTRs: Patellar reflexes 1+, Achilles absent bilaterally.

Skin: Scattered patchy alopecia (expected on docetaxel). No rash, no petechiae, no ecchymosis.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

PSA: 12.1 ng/mL (down from 18.4 ng/mL after cycle 2, and down from 31.7 ng/mL at olaparib discontinuation/chemotherapy initiation). Represents approximately 62% PSA decline from pre-chemotherapy PSA peak.

CBC:
- WBC: 5.4 K/uL (within normal limits; on filgrastim support post-cycle)
- ANC: 3.2 K/uL (adequate)
- Hemoglobin: 9.2 g/dL (Grade 1-2 anemia; stable, last PRBC transfusion approximately 6 weeks ago)
- MCV: 94 fL
- Platelets: 102 K/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, slightly improved from prior)
- Reticulocyte count: 1.1% (modestly improved compared to olaparib period)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138, Potassium 3.9, Chloride 103, Bicarbonate 25 mEq/L
- BUN 18 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 54 mL/min/1.73m2; stable chronic kidney disease)
- Glucose 98 mg/dL, Calcium 9.1 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal)
- ALT: 32 U/L (improved from 48 U/L at last visit, trending toward normal)
- AST: 36 U/L (improved from 52 U/L at last visit)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 142 U/L (mildly elevated, consistent with known bone metastases)
- Albumin: 3.4 g/dL (mildly low, nutritional concern)

Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (castrate).

LDH: 248 U/L (mildly elevated, consistent with metastatic burden).

Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging performed today. Most recent imaging was a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained after cycle 2, which showed a mixed response: the hepatic segment VII lesion decreased from 1.4 to 0.8 cm, the left lower lobe pulmonary nodule was stable at 0.9 cm, bone lesions were stable to minimally improved, and the right external iliac lymph nodes decreased to 1.0 cm. Restaging imaging is planned after completion of cycle 4, which will include CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with consideration of PSMA PET/CT given the PSMA-avid disease characteristics previously confirmed.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC) — BRCA2 biallelic loss, visceral metastases (liver, lung), widespread osseous and nodal disease — PARTIAL RESPONSE TO CARBOPLATIN + DOCETAXEL CHEMOTHERAPY

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old man with heavily pretreated BRCA2-mutant mCRPC who has received three lines of systemic therapy (ADT + abiraterone, enzalutamide, olaparib) before transitioning to carboplatin + docetaxel for visceral disease progression. After 3 cycles of platinum-doublet therapy, he demonstrates a continued PSA response (12.1 ng/mL, 62% decline from pre-chemo peak), improvement of hepatic involvement on prior cycle 2 imaging, and resolution of right upper quadrant discomfort. While the pulmonary nodule remains stable rather than shrinking, the overall trajectory is encouraging. His LFTs continue to improve, consistent with diminishing hepatic tumor burden.

However, he is carrying cumulative toxicity: his anemia (Hgb 9.2 g/dL) remains in the Grade 1-2 range even 6 weeks post-transfusion, his platelet count is borderline-low at 102 K/uL, and his peripheral neuropathy has progressed to Grade 2 involving both hands and feet, now impacting fine motor function and gait stability. Albumin is mildly reduced, suggesting some nutritional vulnerability.

Plan for oncologic management:
- Proceed with cycle 4 of carboplatin + docetaxel (current doses: docetaxel 55 mg/m2 IV, carboplatin AUC 4 IV, day 1 of 21-day cycle).
- Continue filgrastim prophylaxis (days 5-10 post-chemotherapy per protocol).
- Pre-medicate with dexamethasone 8 mg IV, ondansetron 8 mg IV per standard protocol. Dexamethasone also serves to attenuate fluid retention risk with docetaxel (ongoing).
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to be obtained after cycle 4 is completed. Will also discuss PSMA PET/CT at that juncture to re-characterize disease distribution and evaluate suitability for any subsequent line of therapy.
- Continue monitoring PSA trajectory at each visit.

2. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY — GRADE 2, CUMULATIVE (DOCETAXEL + PRIOR PLATINUM EXPOSURE + CARBOPLATIN)

Mr. Kowalski's bilateral hand and foot neuropathy has progressed to Grade 2, with functional impairment (difficulty with fine motor tasks, gait instability). This is a recognized dose-limiting toxicity of docetaxel, compounded by carboplatin and by prior olaparib exposure. Notably, he had Grade 1 neuropathy at baseline, which worsened to Grade 2 during cycle 1.

- Neurology consult has been placed for formal neurologic assessment and electrophysiology evaluation if warranted.
- Physical therapy referral renewed; will focus on balance training and fall prevention given gait instability and Romberg sign.
- Occupational therapy referral placed today for adaptive strategies and assistive devices (large-handle utensils, button aids) given hand involvement.
- No further docetaxel dose reduction at this time (already at 55 mg/m2, 8% reduction from starting dose). Will reassess if neuropathy worsens to Grade 3 or beyond, at which point docetaxel would need to be discontinued.
- Patient advised to avoid hazardous activities (ladder use, sharp objects, extreme temperature exposure to feet) given sensory loss.
- L-glutamine supplementation discussed; patient will consider.

3. ANEMIA (GRADE 1-2, MULTIFACTORIAL — CHEMOTHERAPY-INDUCED + HISTORY OF OLAPARIB-RELATED BONE MARROW SUPPRESSION)

Hemoglobin stable at 9.2 g/dL. Reticulocyte count has improved compared to the olaparib period (now 1.1%), suggesting the bone marrow is recovering some capacity. Patient not symptomatic enough to warrant transfusion today but is being monitored closely. He received 2 PRBC transfusions while on olaparib (at 7 and 9 months) and the last transfusion was 6 weeks ago.

- Continue darbepoetin alfa 500 mcg SC every 3 weeks (targeting Hgb 10-12 g/dL). Recheck Hgb prior to next cycle; if < 8.0 g/dL or symptoms worsen, transfusion threshold will be applied.
- Repeat CBC in 3 weeks (pre-cycle 4 labs).
- Ensure adequate iron stores; ferritin will be rechecked with next labs.
- Dietitian referral placed to address low albumin and ensure adequate caloric/protein intake. High-protein oral nutritional supplements recommended (e.g., Ensure High Protein or Boost High Protein twice daily).

4. THROMBOCYTOPENIA (GRADE 1)

Platelets 102 K/uL — Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, slightly improved from nadir during olaparib period. No active bleeding. Chemotherapy can proceed safely above platelet threshold of 75 K/uL.

- Monitor CBC at each cycle. If platelets fall to <75 K/uL before cycle 4, consider carboplatin dose reduction or delay.
- Patient instructed to report any unusual bruising, bloody stools, or bleeding gums promptly.

5. BONE-TARGETED THERAPY AND BONE HEALTH

Denosumab 120 mg SC every 4 weeks ongoing. Continuing calcium 500 mg and vitamin D 400 IU daily. Last dental evaluation documented approximately 3 months ago; patient reminded to maintain dental hygiene and to alert dental providers of denosumab use (ONJ risk counseling reviewed and documented). Alkaline phosphatase mildly elevated at 142 U/L, consistent with osseous metastatic disease — no specific intervention beyond current bone-targeted therapy.

6. ONGOING ADT — LEUPROLIDE ACETATE

Leuprolide 22.5 mg IM every 3 months continued for castrate testosterone maintenance (last injection documented, next injection due in approximately 6 weeks). Testosterone confirmed castrate at <10 ng/dL today. Patient counseled on ongoing hot flashes (Grade 1, managed with behavioral strategies, no pharmacotherapy initiated at patient's preference), as well as bone density consideration. DEXA scan was not performed during this visit but may be considered given long duration of ADT (>3 years cumulatively).

7. FUNCTIONAL STATUS AND GOALS OF CARE

Mr. Kowalski's ECOG performance status today is assessed as 2 — he is ambulatory and capable of self-care but unable to carry out work activities; he requires some assistance with more vigorous activities. This represents a worsening from his baseline ECOG PS 1 at diagnosis. The decline in functional status reflects cumulative disease burden, neuropathy, and anemia rather than acute decompensation.

The palliative care team has been actively involved and continues to support him and his family with symptom management, advance care planning, and psychosocial support. Per prior discussions, Mr. Kowalski's primary goals are to maintain time at home with his family and to continue treatment as long as it remains tolerable. He and his family have participated in ongoing conversations about realistic expectations of treatment in the mCRPC setting. At this time, he remains engaged with treatment and expresses a desire to continue.

Social work is involved. He lives with his adult daughter, who serves as his primary caregiver. Transportation to clinic has been arranged through the hospital's patient transportation service.

8. INFECTION PROPHYLAXIS / SUPPORTIVE CARE

- Continuing filgrastim G-CSF prophylaxis given prior Grade 3 neutropenia, ECOG PS 2, and age >65.
- Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole single-strength Monday/Wednesday/Friday for PCP prophylaxis (on dexamethasone premedication regimen and prior prolonged steroid use with abiraterone; low-dose ongoing).
- Antiemetics: ondansetron 8 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for 3 days post-chemotherapy; prochlorperazine 10 mg PO PRN as breakthrough. Patient has been tolerating this regimen with adequate nausea control (Grade 1 currently).

FOLLOW-UP

Return to clinic in 3 weeks for pre-cycle 4 labs (CBC, CMP, PSA) and cycle 4 of carboplatin + docetaxel. Restaging CT to be scheduled post-cycle 4. Patient given urgent care number and instructed to call if fever > 38.3°C, severe bleeding, worsening neurologic symptoms (new weakness, urinary/bowel incontinence), or any new acute symptoms arise.

Time spent with patient and family: approximately 45 minutes. Patient and daughter verbalized understanding of the plan and had opportunity to ask questions.

---

Electronically signed:
[Attending Genitourinary Oncologist]
Genitourinary Medical Oncology
[Institution]
